coaching.coaching: Test the riskiest assumption behind lizard and jscpd wiring into code_health specs first.
coaching.subject: lizard and jscpd wiring into code_health specs

coaching.coaching: Weigh at least one structural alternative for cross-ecosystem binary delivery mechanism for pip extras.
coaching.subject: cross-ecosystem binary delivery mechanism for pip extras

coaching.coaching: Pin down acceptance-criteria completeness and measurability with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: acceptance-criteria completeness and measurability

coaching.coaching: Plan how aggregator child integration of upstream analyzer outputs will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: aggregator child integration of upstream analyzer outputs

coaching.coaching: Run a quick pre-mortem on existing consumer breakage without rollback path: how could this plan fail?
coaching.subject: existing consumer breakage without rollback path

coaching.coaching: Generalize shared constant duplication across child modules across the rest of the work.
coaching.subject: shared constant duplication across child modules

coaching.coaching: Research prior art / OSS for external consumers of deregistered cap_change_events metric before building it custom.
coaching.subject: external consumers of deregistered cap_change_events metric

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and register MetricSpec entries (or extend _analyzer_spec) in 0734 (or a dedicated child) that wire lizard complexity and jscpd duplication results into the code_health metric lens, covering the remainder left by scc-only wiring.
evidence: Parent AC1: 'code_health metrics computed via the analyzer stack (scc/lizard/jscpd) for a Python client AND at least one non-Python client'
Parent Design: 'analyze(repo_root, languages) -> {loc, complexity, duplication}'
0734 Mechanism §1: '_analyzer_spec compute(ctx) calls scc_loc.analyze(ctx.repo_root, ctx.scan_roots)' — only scc_loc, no lizard or jscpd
0734 Mechanism §3: fns over loc payload only — no complexity or duplication fn defined
21fb AC: 'analyzers/lizard_complexity.py runs lizard -> per-file/aggregate CCN' — but no child consumes this output in a registered MetricSpec
3ba0 AC: 'analyze(root) returns .duplication.clones' — but no child wires this into the code_health metric registry
findings.finding: Parent AC1 requires code_health metrics computed via the full analyzer stack (scc/lizard/jscpd), but only scc is wired into the registered code_health metric specs by any child. Children 21fb (lizard/complexity) and 3ba0 (jscpd/duplication) build adapters that are never wired into the code_health metric lens — no child defines metric specs or adapter calls for complexity or duplication data in the code_health output.
findings.location: child 0734-ceae-be55-4fa7 (Mechanism section); child 21fb-f4cd-3eaa-4b60; child 3ba0-9350-8337-429a
scenarios: A reviewer checks that scc/lizard/jscpd all contribute to code_health output — complexity and duplication values are absent from any registered metric
The epic completion verifier checks for a single code_health API response containing loc + complexity + duplication — it will only find loc
a532 E2E test validates 'code_health computes for BOTH languages' but 0734 only wires loc, so the test passes with partial data and the parent AC is silently under-delivered
findings.suggested_fix: Either extend 0734's mechanism to add complexity/duplication fns over AnalyzerResult.complexity and AnalyzerResult.duplication (calling all three analyzers in _analyzer_spec or a composite analyze() call), or create a new child ticket that wires 21fb and 3ba0 outputs into registered MetricSpec entries, ensuring all three tools contribute to the code_health lens output.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Design section that `pip install rebar[metrics]` delivers only the Python analyzer layer (lizard + adapter code); scc (Go binary) and jscpd (npm) are system-level prerequisites that must be listed alongside `git` in the install docs, and the `Unavailable` message for each missing tool should name the specific install command.
evidence: 'Package tools + analyzer under an optional [metrics] extra' — implies pip delivers all three tools
scc is described as '(MIT, Go)' — Go binaries are not pip-installable
jscpd is currently used via `npx jscpd` (docs/maintenance-audit-runbook.md line 26, 65)
Existing codebase treats scc/lizard/jscpd as external CLIs checked via shutil.which (engine_b.py:60 _METRIC_CANDIDATES, engine_b_backends.py:346)
pyproject.toml documents git as a system prerequisite, not a pip dep — the same pattern applies here
findings.finding: The plan states 'Package tools + analyzer under an optional [metrics] extra' implying `pip install rebar[metrics]` delivers scc, lizard, and jscpd. However, scc is a Go binary (installable via `go install` or platform package managers, not pip) and jscpd is an npm package (installable via `npm install -g jscpd` or `npx jscpd`). Only lizard is pip-installable. The packaging mechanism is broken for two of the three tools: a `[metrics]` extra can deliver lizard as a Python dep, but scc and jscpd must be documented as system-level prerequisites (like `git` already is), not pip deps. If implemented as written, a user who runs `pip install rebar[metrics]` will still be missing scc and jscpd with no clear error at install time.
findings.location: Design section: 'Package tools + analyzer under an optional [metrics] extra; core install stays dependency-free'; Recommended default stack section (scc/jscpd descriptions)
scenarios: A client runs `pip install nava-rebar[metrics]` expecting to get all three tools; lizard installs but scc and jscpd are absent; analyzer silently falls back to Unavailable with no install-time warning that two tools are missing.
CI pipeline installs the [metrics] extra but has no Go toolchain; scc is absent; the AC requiring metrics for a Go client project cannot be satisfied.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Design section add: 'The [metrics] pip extra delivers lizard (Python) and the adapter/interface code. scc and jscpd are system-level CLIs — document them as prerequisites (e.g. `brew install scc` / `npm install -g jscpd`) alongside `git`, and ensure the Unavailable reason for each missing tool names its install command.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix AC#2 and the Design section to accurately state that `pip install rebar[metrics]` installs only the Python-installable tools (lizard); scc (Go) and jscpd (npm) are external system/runtime preconditions that must be documented separately, not pip dependencies.
evidence: AC#2: 'analyzer + tools live behind pip install rebar[metrics]'
scc is described as a Go binary — Go binaries are not installable via pip
jscpd is a Node.js package; docs/maintenance-audit-runbook.md:26 confirms it is used as 'npx jscpd', not a Python dep
pyproject.toml [grounding] extra (line 144) only contains 'tree-sitter' Python packages — confirming the project's existing pattern is: Python-only extras, external CLIs are system preconditions
Evidence class: Contradicted — the packaging claim is falsified by the tool runtimes
findings.finding: The plan asserts that scc (a Go binary) and jscpd (a Node.js package) can be shipped as part of `pip install rebar[metrics]`, but neither can be declared as a Python package dependency in pyproject.toml `[project.optional-dependencies]`. The maintenance runbook confirms jscpd is used as `npx jscpd` (an npm precondition), not a pip dep. AC#2 ('analyzer + tools live behind `pip install rebar[metrics]`') is therefore technically infeasible as written for two of the three tools.
findings.location: AC#2 ('Zero new HARD deps in rebar core; analyzer + tools live behind `pip install rebar[metrics]`') and Recommended stack section ('scc (MIT, Go)' / 'jscpd (MIT)')
scenarios: A client runs `pip install rebar[metrics]` expecting all tools to be present; scc and jscpd are absent because they require separate Go/npm installation steps not described anywhere
CI environments that have Python but not Go or Node fail silently at runtime because the extra promises more than pip can deliver
findings.suggested_fix: Restate AC#2 as: 'The `[metrics]` extra installs lizard; scc and jscpd are documented external prerequisites (Go binary / npx). Core-only install degrades to Unavailable with a clear message naming which tool is missing.' Add an installation guide section for non-Python tools.

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin the required non-Python client language for AC1 (replace 'e.g. Go or TS' with a specific language and a concrete test fixture reference) so the criterion is unambiguously evaluable.
evidence: 'code_health metrics computed via the analyzer stack (scc/lizard/jscpd) for a Python client AND at least one non-Python client (e.g. Go or TS)'
'e.g.' signals a suggestion, not a decision
findings.finding: AC1 specifies 'at least one non-Python client (e.g. Go or TS)' without pinning which one. For a CI test fixture, the 'e.g.' leaves the executor to choose arbitrarily, which could result in a trivial or untested scenario.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria / AC1
scenarios: Executor picks the easiest non-Python fixture (e.g. a single .go file) that technically satisfies the AC but doesn't validate the full polyglot path

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the non-Python language fixture in 21fb's AC (e.g., 'Go fixture at tests/fixtures/metrics_polyglot/go_project/') and coordinate it with the fixture used by a532 to ensure the same non-Python language is covered end-to-end.
evidence: 21fb AC: 'Exercised on Python + one non-Python fixture (contributes toward the epics cross-language AC)'
21fb Verification: 'Unit over Python + one other language fixture; guard-absent -> Unavailable' — 'one other language fixture' is unspecified
Parent AC1: 'at least one non-Python client (e.g. Go or TS)' — example languages given but child does not commit to either
a532 does name Go/TS as examples but 21fb's own test fixture is not coordinated with a532's fixture
findings.finding: Child 21fb's acceptance criteria specify lizard is 'exercised on Python + one non-Python fixture' but does not name the fixture type, language, or location — unlike 18f9 (which uses a canned scc JSON) and 3ba0 (which uses a known-clone fixture). This scope-narrowing relative to the parent's AC1 requirement ('at least one non-Python client, e.g. Go or TS') leaves the non-Python fixture underspecified and risks the test covering only Python in practice.
findings.location: child 21fb-f4cd-3eaa-4b60 (Acceptance criteria, third bullet)
scenarios: Developer writing test_lizard_complexity.py picks an arbitrary non-Python language (e.g. C) that differs from a532's Go/TS fixture, making cross-child consistency checks harder
A completion verifier checks 21fb's test in isolation and cannot confirm the non-Python fixture type actually matches the epic's target client languages
findings.suggested_fix: Update 21fb's AC to name a concrete non-Python language (e.g., Go) and reference the shared fixture directory created by a532 (tests/fixtures/metrics_polyglot/go_project/), so the lizard complexity test reuses the same fixture as the E2E validation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either remove 21fb and 3ba0 from 0734's depends_on list (if 0734 truly does not consume them) and create a new integration child that does, or add mechanism steps and ACs to 0734 that actually wire and test lizard/jscpd outputs.
evidence: 0734 Dependencies: '3b30, 18f9, 21fb, 3ba0, e7a0 (recorded depends_on)'
0734 Mechanism §1: compute(ctx) calls 'scc_loc.analyze(ctx.repo_root, ctx.scan_roots)' only — no reference to lizard_complexity or jscpd_dup anywhere in the mechanism
0734 ACs: all seven proving commands reference only size/loc metrics (module_size_distribution, oversized_module_count) — none validate complexity or duplication output
findings.finding: Child 0734 lists 21fb and 3ba0 as depends_on edges, implying it consumes their outputs, but its Mechanism section has no step that calls lizard_complexity.analyze() or jscpd_dup.analyze(). 0734 can pass all its own acceptance criteria without 21fb or 3ba0 being merged, making the dependency edges misleading and potentially triggering a false epic-completion signal.
findings.location: child 0734-ceae-be55-4fa7 (Dependencies line and Mechanism section)
scenarios: A release manager sees 0734 green + 21fb green + 3ba0 green and marks the epic complete, unaware that complexity/duplication were never wired into the metrics lens
A dependency-graph tool marks 21fb/3ba0 as blocking 0734, causing scheduling delays for work that is not actually needed by 0734
findings.suggested_fix: Remove 21fb and 3ba0 from 0734's depends_on edges. Create a new child ticket 'Wire complexity and duplication adapters into code_health metric specs' that depends_on 0734+21fb+3ba0 and adds the missing MetricSpec registrations.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (or expand-contract sequence) for the removal of the old hardcoded readers: e.g., a deprecation release that emits Unavailable with a 'migrate by adding analyzer config' message, plus documented steps to revert to the prior readers if the new stack regresses.
evidence: 'Hard fallback contract: if no analyzer is configured for the client's language, emit a genuine Unavailable(...)' — existing consumers who haven't configured the analyzer will silently lose metric output

Plan acknowledges the audit finding and describes the old behavior as 'silent-misleading-data' but provides no expand-contract steps (e.g., deprecate-then-remove), no feature flag to re-enable old behavior during transition, and no rollback instructions if the new stack regresses

T4 rubric: 'The remedy for a destructive/breaking change is an explicit ROLLBACK / back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing — checking only that breakage is acknowledged is insufficient; require the reversibility mechanism'
findings.finding: The plan breaks existing consumers of module_size_distribution / cap_change_events (they will receive Unavailable instead of their current data, however misleading) without providing a rollback path or expand-contract sequencing — the breakage is justified but the reversibility mechanism is absent.
findings.location: Design section / Hard fallback contract bullet; Acceptance criteria line 4
scenarios: A team running rebar against a Python project today receives module_size_distribution data; after this change they get Unavailable until they add analyzer config — no migration guide tells them what config to add or how to revert if the new output is wrong
If the new analyzer stack produces incorrect results for an existing client, there is no documented back-out path to restore the previous (even flawed) readers while a fix is developed
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Migration & rollback' section to the plan: (1) in a prior release, wrap old readers to emit a DeprecationWarning + still-valid data; (2) in this release, switch to Unavailable; (3) document that re-pinning to the prior rebar release restores old behavior as a rollback escape hatch; (4) add a migration guide snippet showing the minimal per-project config that replicates the old Python/rebar behavior.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Design or a dedicated migration note whether lizard and jscpd are to be MOVED from their current dependency location to the `[metrics]` extra, or remain as-is (and if moved, note any core-code migration required).
evidence: 'lizard (MIT, Python) — TRUE cyclomatic complexity... Already a rebar dev/backfill dep.'
'jscpd (MIT) — duplication/clone detection... Already a rebar dep.'
'Package tools + analyzer under an optional [metrics] extra; core install stays dependency-free'
AC #2: 'Zero new HARD deps in rebar core; analyzer + tools live behind pip install rebar[metrics]'
findings.finding: The 'Recommended stack' section labels lizard as 'Already a rebar dev/backfill dep' and jscpd as 'Already a rebar dep', yet the Design section and AC #2 state these tools must live exclusively behind the `[metrics]` extra so 'core install gains no new hard dependencies'. This creates a contradiction: are lizard and jscpd being MOVED from their current dep location to the extra (a breaking change), or are they simply reused in-place (in which case the 'no new deps in core' claim is vacuous for them)? An implementer cannot tell whether a migration of existing deps is required.
findings.location: Recommended stack section ('Already a rebar dep' / 'Already a rebar dev/backfill dep') vs. Design section ('Package tools + analyzer under an optional [metrics] extra') and AC #2
scenarios: Implementer leaves lizard/jscpd in core deps (they're 'already there') and AC #2 still passes trivially, defeating the portability goal
Implementer moves them to extras, breaking any existing core code that imports them, with no migration guidance in the plan
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note such as: 'lizard and jscpd are currently listed as dev/backfill deps; this plan promotes them to optional [metrics] extras and removes them from the core/dev dependency set. Any existing core imports must be guarded with try/except ImportError -> Unavailable.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the doc AC to explicitly require auditing and updating (or retiring) existing documentation that references module_size_distribution, cap_change_events, and .github/module-size-limit.txt so no stale references remain after the change.
evidence: Plan names 'module_size_distribution / cap_change_events / module-size seeds' and '.github/module-size-limit.txt' as current artifacts being replaced — any existing user-guide or README sections referencing these will be stale
Doc AC only says 'describe the extra, config, and the fallback contract' — no mention of auditing/updating existing docs that reference the old behavior or governance file
T7 rubric: 'INVALIDATED — does the change make existing docs/references stale (deleted/renamed artifacts still referenced)'
findings.finding: The plan's doc AC covers new artifacts but does not address existing documentation that references the removed hardcoded paths and old metric names (module_size_distribution, cap_change_events, .github/module-size-limit.txt), which will become stale references after the change.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — 'Docs (reuse-surface, user-guide) describe the extra, config, and the fallback contract'
scenarios: A user reads the existing user-guide section on module_size_distribution, follows the old instructions, and gets Unavailable with no explanation because the doc hasn't been updated to describe the new config requirement
The rebar governance doc referencing .github/module-size-limit.txt is no longer accurate but is not flagged for update
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC: 'Existing docs/references to module_size_distribution, cap_change_events, and .github/module-size-limit.txt are updated or removed; a redirect/note points users to the new per-project config pattern.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define _ACCRUING_SINCE in a single canonical location (e.g., rebar.metrics.analyzers or rebar.metrics._constants) and import it in scc_loc.py, jscpd_dup.py, git_metrics.py, and event_metrics.py instead of each defining its own copy.
evidence: 18f9: '_ACCRUING_SINCE = "2026-01-01T00:00:00+00:00" — the SAME module-level ISO-8601 constant value used by the existing metrics reader modules'
3ba0: 'accruing_since is a module-level _ACCRUING_SINCE constant (matching existing reader modules), passed to Unavailable(reason, accruing_since)'
0734 §5: 'accruing_since="2026-01-01T00:00:00+00:00" (the value the existing metrics reader modules use)'
No child designates a canonical import location for this constant; each defines its own copy
findings.finding: The _ACCRUING_SINCE constant ('2026-01-01T00:00:00+00:00') is independently defined as a module-level copy in at least three children (18f9 in scc_loc.py, 3ba0 in jscpd_dup.py, and retained in git_metrics.py by 0734), with no single source of truth. If this value needs updating, multiple modules diverge silently.
findings.location: child 18f9-5b75-b909-408c (Concrete contracts section); child 3ba0-9350-8337-429a (Approach section); child 0734-ceae-be55-4fa7 (Provenance §5)
scenarios: The accruing_since boundary needs correction post-launch — developer updates git_metrics.py but misses scc_loc.py and jscpd_dup.py, causing different modules to report different accruing_since values for the same lens
A metrics consumer diffs accruing_since across code_health sub-metrics and finds inconsistency
findings.suggested_fix: Add a rebar/metrics/_constants.py (or similar) exporting ACCRUING_SINCE, and have all reader/adapter modules import from it. This is a small additive change compatible with 3b30 being the keystone.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before deregistering cap_change_events, add a grep/audit step (or AC) in 0734 confirming no CI workflow, script, or dashboard references this metric ID, or document a migration path for any found consumers.
evidence: 0734 Mechanism §4: 'DEREGISTER cap_change_events (remove from register())'
0734 Problem: 'Does NOT touch the CI module-size GATE (separate governance)'
Parent Why: 'cap_change_events... hardcode .github/module-size-limit.txt (a rebar governance artifact)' — implies it is used in rebar's own CI
No child contains a consumer enumeration for cap_change_events (grep of dependents, CI workflow audit, API consumer check)
findings.finding: Child 0734 deregisters cap_change_events from the metrics registry, but no child audits whether any existing CI script, dashboard, or external consumer currently polls this metric ID. The parent notes 'CI module-size GATE is separate governance' but does not confirm the gate does not consume cap_change_events via the rebar metrics API.
findings.location: child 0734-ceae-be55-4fa7 (Mechanism §4: DEREGISTER cap_change_events)
scenarios: The CI module-size gate calls rebar metrics and checks for cap_change_events in the output; after 0734 lands, the gate silently receives no data and stops enforcing the cap
A team dashboard tracking cap_change_events stops updating without an alert
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC to 0734: 'PROVE: grep -rE "cap_change_events" .github/ scripts/ docs/ returns no hits (or enumerate and document each hit with a migration plan).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the explicit delivery mechanism for non-Python tools (scc/jscpd) in the [metrics] extra — e.g., declare which PyPI shim packages to use (such as `scc` on PyPI for the Go binary, `jscpd` via a Node shim), or specify that the extra documents a system-level prerequisite and shells out via PATH, with a clear Unavailable fallback when the binary is absent.
evidence: 'Package tools + analyzer under an optional [metrics] extra; core install stays dependency-free'
scc is described as 'MIT, Go' — not a Python package
jscpd is described as 'MIT' and is an npm package, not installable via pip
Absence: no mention of shim packages (e.g. scc-python on PyPI), subprocess PATH assumptions, or bundling strategy
findings.finding: The delivery mechanism for non-Python tools (scc is a Go binary; jscpd is an npm/Node package) via `pip install rebar[metrics]` is never specified. An executor cannot determine whether to use PyPI shim packages, system-level package managers, Docker images, or some other mechanism — this is a blocking ambiguity.
findings.location: Design section / 'Package tools + analyzer under an optional [metrics] extra'
scenarios: Executor writes setup.cfg/pyproject.toml [metrics] extras but cannot add scc or jscpd as pip dependencies without a PyPI shim or explicit bundling decision
CI environment may lack Go or Node runtimes, making shell-out to scc/jscpd silently fail
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Tool delivery' subsection under Design: 'scc will be invoked as a subprocess expected on PATH; jscpd likewise. The [metrics] extra lists `lizard>=1.23` as a pip dependency (pure Python). For scc and jscpd, document in the extra's README that users must install them via their native package managers (brew/apt/go install/npm -g). The analyzer checks for binary presence at startup and emits Unavailable("scc not found on PATH; install via ...") if absent.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the concrete return schema of `analyze()` in the plan — at minimum specify field types, units, and aggregation level for each of `loc`, `complexity`, and `duplication` (e.g., `loc: dict[str, int]` keyed by language with a `'total'` key; `complexity: float` as mean CCN across all functions; `duplication: float` as percentage of duplicated lines).
evidence: 'analyze(repo_root, languages) -> {loc, complexity, duplication}'
Absence: no TypedDict, dataclass, or prose description of field units (e.g., is loc an int total or dict[lang, int]? is complexity average CCN or max CCN?)
findings.finding: The return type of `analyze(repo_root, languages) -> {loc, complexity, duplication}` has no defined schema: units, aggregation level (per-file? per-language? totals?), and field types are unstated. An executor must invent the data contract.
findings.location: Design section / 'Define an analyzer interface: analyze(repo_root, languages) -> {loc, complexity, duplication}'
scenarios: Different implementers produce incompatible schemas for the same interface
Downstream metric consumers (module_size_distribution, cap_change_events) cannot be updated deterministically without knowing the shape of the return value

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the provenance of the `languages` argument: specify whether it comes from per-project config (declared by the user), scc auto-detection output, or both (config overrides auto-detect), so the executor can implement the call site correctly.
evidence: 'analyze(repo_root, languages) -> {loc, complexity, duplication}'
'Per-project config selects tool + thresholds'
Absence: no statement of where the languages list originates
findings.finding: The `languages` parameter of `analyze(repo_root, languages)` has no stated provenance — the plan does not specify whether language detection is performed by scc auto-detect, declared in per-project config, inferred from file extensions, or passed by the caller. An executor cannot determine how to populate this argument.
findings.location: Design section / 'analyze(repo_root, languages)'
scenarios: Executor defaults to scc auto-detection but per-project config was intended to override it, causing incorrect language scoping
Client with mixed-language repo (e.g., Python + JS) gets wrong tool selected

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a minimal config schema example to the plan (file location, format, and required/optional field names) so the executor does not need to invent the configuration surface.
evidence: 'Per-project config selects tool + thresholds'
'scan root + size policy are per-project config (not hardcoded)'
Absence: no config file name, format (TOML/YAML/Python), or field names specified
findings.finding: The per-project config schema — where it lives and what fields it contains — is never specified. 'Per-project config selects tool + thresholds' and 'scan root + size policy are per-project config' require the executor to invent a config format.
findings.location: Design section / 'Per-project config selects tool + thresholds; the rebar module-size CAP becomes one optional policy layered on the generic LOC signal'
scenarios: Executor chooses pyproject.toml [tool.rebar.metrics] but the intended design was a separate rebar.yaml
Two contributors implement incompatible config keys for the same setting
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Config schema' subsection: 'rebar config (pyproject.toml under [tool.rebar.metrics]) with fields: scan_root (str, default "."), languages (list[str], default auto-detect), analyzer (str, default "scc+lizard+jscpd"), module_size_cap_loc (int|null, default null=disabled).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'reuse-surface' in the plan, add a Documentation section describing what each doc artifact must contain (e.g. which pages to update, what the user-guide entry for the [metrics] extra must cover), and ensure AC #6's verify step references those concrete artifacts.
evidence: AC #6 text: 'Docs (reuse-surface, user-guide) describe the extra, config, and the fallback contract.'
ABSENCE: No section in Goal, Why, Design, or Out of Scope mentions documentation work, defines 'reuse-surface', or describes what the user-guide update entails.
findings.finding: AC #6 references two documentation deliverables — 'reuse-surface' docs and a 'user-guide' — that have no corresponding description anywhere in the plan body (Goal, Why, Design, or elsewhere). The term 'reuse-surface' is never defined, and the plan gives no guidance on what the user-guide must cover, making this criterion unverifiable and an orphaned requirement.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria, AC #6: 'Docs (reuse-surface, user-guide) describe the extra, config, and the fallback contract.'
scenarios: Reviewer cannot verify AC #6 because 'reuse-surface' is undefined
Implementer skips docs work because the plan provides no spec for it, then AC #6 is checked off superficially

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a pre-build spike or RED integration fixture that runs the full scc+lizard+jscpd shell-out pipeline on at least one Python repo AND one non-Python (Go or TS) repo, asserting the normalized `{loc, complexity, duplication}` output is consistent and non-fabricated before the full implementation is committed.
evidence: 'Define an analyzer interface: analyze(repo_root, languages) -> {loc, complexity, duplication}, shelling out to the configured tool(s)' — asserted, not prototyped
AC1 requires non-Python client correctness as an acceptance criterion, not a prior validation step
No spike, probe, RED test, or pilot mentioned anywhere in the plan
The normalization of three tools' differing output schemas is the project's OWN novel code path, not an advertised library contract
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel multi-tool shell-out composition layer (scc + lizard + jscpd -> normalized {loc, complexity, duplication}) and asserts it works correctly for non-Python clients, but includes no spike, RED fixture, prototype, or empirical probe to validate that the normalization logic produces consistent, correct output across tool combinations before full build-out.
findings.location: Design section ('Define an analyzer interface') and AC1 ('code_health metrics computed via the analyzer stack … for at least one non-Python client')
scenarios: scc and lizard report LOC with different whitespace/comment accounting; the composed metric silently reports inconsistent or double-counted values for a Go repo
jscpd's duplication output schema differs between v4 and v5 in a way that breaks the normalizer only on TS files with JSX
The non-Python client integration is only discovered to be broken at AC acceptance, requiring rework of the interface contract
findings.suggested_fix: Create a `spike/analyzer_probe.py` (or equivalent) that: (1) runs each tool on a small fixture repo in both Python and Go/TS, (2) captures raw output schemas, (3) asserts the normalizer produces matching units and non-zero, non-fabricated values, and (4) documents any per-language discrepancies. Gate the Design section's interface contract on the spike's findings.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or AGENTS.md section capturing: (a) the rationale for the optional-extra boundary vs. a plugin system, (b) the hard fallback contract invariants ('NEVER fabricate zeros, NEVER leak errno'), and (c) the normalization interface contract for the multi-tool analyzer.
evidence: AC6 lists 'reuse-surface, user-guide' only — no mention of ADR, AGENTS.md, or design doc

The optional-extra boundary, the hard fallback contract ('NEVER fabricate zeros, NEVER leak errno'), and the multi-tool normalization interface are novel architectural decisions not captured in any design record
ABSENCE: no 'ADR', 'AGENTS.md', 'design doc', or 'architecture decision' appears anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel optional-extra + polyglot analyzer interface + hard fallback contract — a meaningful architectural decision — but the only documentation called out (AC6) is user-facing docs (reuse-surface, user-guide); no ADR or AGENTS.md update is planned to capture the design rationale and constraints for future maintainers.
findings.location: AC6 ('Docs (reuse-surface, user-guide) describe the extra, config, and the fallback contract') and Design section
scenarios: A future maintainer adds a fourth tool or a new language and is unaware of the 'NEVER fabricate zeros' invariant because it lives only in a user guide, not in a developer-facing design doc
The optional-extra pattern is inconsistently applied to a future metric because the decision rationale (why extras, not a plugin system) was never recorded

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit sequencing note in the parent plan (or in nirvanic-hydrated-falcon) stating that the analyzer interface + fallback contract must be merged and agreed before the scc/lizard/jscpd adapter tickets are started.
evidence: No sequencing statement in parent plan
Interface ticket 'nirvanic-hydrated-falcon' gates the adapter contracts but is listed as a peer sibling with no stated prerequisite
Rubric: 'Sequencing has a thin vertical-slice / evidence-gated MVP de-risking the riskiest piece first'
findings.finding: The parent plan does not state an explicit sequencing constraint that the analyzer interface ticket (nirvanic-hydrated-falcon) must land before the three adapter tickets (scc/lizard/jscpd) begin; if adapters are started concurrently the interface API may shift and force rework across all three.
findings.location: Design section / child ticket list (nirvanic-hydrated-falcon vs. bovid-ambient-bactrian, injured-silver-merlin, old-improvised-herring)
scenarios: Three adapter authors start in parallel against an unstable interface; the interface changes partway through; all three adapters require rework on the return-type or error-model.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Design section, add: 'Sequencing: nirvanic-hydrated-falcon (interface + fallback contract) must merge first; bovid-ambient-bactrian, injured-silver-merlin, and old-improvised-herring are unblocked only after the interface is stable.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC2 and the Docs AC to explicitly distinguish pip-installable deps (lizard only) from externally-installed CLIs (scc via Go toolchain/release binary, jscpd via npm/npx), and document the separate install path for each non-Python tool.
evidence: 'Package tools + analyzer under an optional [metrics] extra; core install stays dependency-free' — plan conflates pip extras with binary tool delivery
scc is distributed as a Go binary (GitHub releases / brew / apt); jscpd is distributed via npm (`npx jscpd`); only lizard is a PyPI package

Existing usage in repo (backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py, docs/maintenance-audit-runbook.md) invokes jscpd via `npx jscpd` and scc as PATH CLIs — consistent with external install, not pip-managed
pyproject.toml extras (grounding, tracing) only list PyPI packages; there is no existing mechanism for shipping Go binaries or npm tools via pip extras
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'analyzer + tools live behind `pip install rebar[metrics]`' (AC2) but scc is a Go binary and jscpd is an npm package — neither is pip-installable. The plan makes no mention of how these non-Python tool binaries are actually delivered to the user's PATH when they run `pip install rebar[metrics]`, leaving the core delivery mechanism unverified.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — AC2 ('analyzer + tools live behind `pip install rebar[metrics]`'); Recommended default stack section
scenarios: A client runs `pip install rebar[metrics]` expecting scc and jscpd to become available, but only lizard is installed; scc and jscpd remain absent and the analyzer reports Unavailable.
CI environment has pip but not Go/npm toolchains; the [metrics] extra installs lizard but scc and jscpd are still missing, making the extra misleading.
findings.suggested_fix: Split AC2 into two parts: (a) `pip install rebar[metrics]` installs lizard and registers the analyzer entry-point; (b) scc and jscpd must be installed separately (document OS-package/npm commands). The Docs AC should explicitly list the install command for each tool by platform.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief prior-art note in the Design section explicitly addressing the existing scc/lizard/jscpd integrations in `engine_b_backends.py` and `backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py`, and state whether the new analyzer interface will replace, extend, or coexist with them.
evidence: src/rebar/grounding/engine_b_backends.py lines 367-436: `_metric_invoke` already shells to scc with `--format json` and parses `Code`/`Location` fields from its JSON output
scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py lines 47-83: already invokes lizard (text output, CCN column) and jscpd (`--reporters json`, `statistics.total.clones`)
Plan says 'Define an analyzer interface ... shelling out to the configured tool(s)' without referencing these existing implementations
ABSENCE: plan mentions no consolidation plan for the existing backfill script or engine_b metric backend
findings.finding: The plan does not acknowledge that scc (via `_metric_invoke`/`_scc_files`) and jscpd (via `backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py`) are already integrated in the codebase with their own parsing logic and output-schema assumptions. Without engaging this prior art, the plan risks producing a second, parallel implementation that diverges from or conflicts with the existing one.
findings.location: Design section ('Define an analyzer interface'); Recommended default stack section
scenarios: Two separate scc JSON parsers with different field assumptions exist post-implementation; a scc version bump breaks one but not the other.
The new analyzer interface duplicates the engine_b metric backend without the plan calling for their unification, leaving dead code.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that the asserted tool versions (scc v4.x, lizard v1.23, jscpd v5) produce output schemas compatible with the parsing code already in the repo (specifically `_scc_files()` field names, jscpd `statistics.total.clones` path, and lizard CCN column index), and document the version floor in the [metrics] extra or install instructions.
evidence: 'scc (MIT, Go) — LOC + fast size/DRYness + approx complexity + COCOMO. v4.x, 8.5k stars.'
'lizard (MIT, Python) — TRUE cyclomatic complexity (CCN) + token/param counts across ~25 languages. v1.23 (2026).'
'jscpd (MIT) — duplication/clone detection, 223 formats, Rabin-Karp. v5.'
Existing scc parser in engine_b_backends.py uses `Code` and `Location` fields — not confirmed stable across all v4.x
jscpd v3→v4→v5 have historically changed reporter output format (e.g., the `statistics` key structure changed between major versions)
findings.finding: The plan makes specific version assertions ('jscpd v5', 'lizard v1.23 (2026)', 'scc v4.x') but does not verify that these versions' CLI interfaces and output schemas are compatible with the parsing logic already present in the repo (scc `--format json` producing `Code`/`Location` fields; jscpd `--reporters json` producing `statistics.total.clones`; lizard text output with CCN column at index 1).
findings.location: Recommended default stack section — version assertions for each tool
scenarios: jscpd v5 changes the JSON report path or `statistics.total.clones` key; existing adapter silently returns None instead of raising an error.
A future scc v4.x point release renames the `Code` field; the existing parser silently counts zero oversize files.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact lizard CLI invocation and output format (e.g., `lizard <root> --xml` or column-index parsing) that the `analyze()` interface will use, and verify token/param extraction works with lizard v1.23 before committing to those fields in the interface contract.
evidence: scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py:60-64: lizard stdout parsed by column index — 'parts[0].isdigit() and parts[1].isdigit()' — no structured output flag used
Plan claims 'token/param counts' but existing code only extracts CCN (col[1]) — token/param parsing is not implemented and the flag to obtain them in structured form is unspecified
Lizard v1.23 (claimed) — specific flags for structured output not confirmed against --help
Evidence class: Partially-verified (CCN via column parsing works; token/param via structured output is Unverified)
findings.finding: The plan names `lizard` as providing 'TRUE cyclomatic complexity (CCN) + token/param counts' but does not specify the CLI invocation pattern or output format for the new `analyze()` interface. Existing code parses lizard's tabular text stdout using column-index heuristics (col[0].isdigit(), col[1] = CCN) — a fragile, undocumented format. If the new interface wants structured output, lizard supports `--xml` but that flag is unverified against the version pinned (v1.23). Flag-level mismatch risk exists for any deviation from the column-parsing approach.
findings.location: Recommended stack section ('lizard — TRUE cyclomatic complexity (CCN) + token/param counts') and Design section ('shelling out to the configured tool(s)')
scenarios: Lizard v1.23 changes column ordering or output header format slightly, breaking the column-index parser silently
The new `analyze()` interface needs param counts but the implementer must reverse-engineer lizard's output format from scratch because the plan doesn't specify flags

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
